data.title: Is Prudential Financial (PRU) Pricing Reflect Its Value After Recent Share Price Volatility?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Prudential Financial's current share price reflects its true worth, starting with a clear look at valuation can help you decide how it fits in your portfolio.
  • The stock last closed at US$99.42, with returns of 2.9% over 7 days, 6.7% over 30 days, a negative 12.7% year to date, 6.2% over 1 year, 31.5% over 3 years, and 29.1% over 5 years, giving you a mixed picture of recent and longer term performance.
  • Recent moves in Prudential Financial's share price sit against a backdrop of ongoing market discussion around large U.S. insurers and their sensitivity to interest rates and capital requirements. Broader sector headlines about regulation, capital strength, and product risk are an important backdrop for thinking about what investors are currently willing to pay for this stock.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Prudential Financial scores 5 out of 6. The rest of this article breaks down how different valuation approaches arrive at that score, and then finishes with a way to think about valuation that goes beyond any single model.

Prudential Financial delivered 6.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Insurance industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Prudential Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how effectively Prudential Financial turns its equity base into earnings above the return that investors are assumed to require. It compares the return generated on book value to the estimated cost of equity and then capitalizes those “excess” profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Prudential Financial, book value is estimated at $93.23 per share, with a stable earnings per share estimate of $14.54, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 11 analysts. That implies an average Return on Equity of 13.34%. The model assumes a cost of equity of $8.70 per share, which results in an excess return of $5.84 per share.

The stable book value is projected at $108.97 per share, drawn from weighted future Book Value estimates from 7 analysts. Combining this equity base with the excess return assumptions produces an intrinsic value estimate of about $236.47 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $99.42, this Excess Returns model suggests the stock is 58.0% undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Prudential Financial is undervalued by 58.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Prudential Financial.

Approach 2: Prudential Financial Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it directly ties what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E ratio usually reflects a mix of expectations and risk. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk tends to align with a lower P/E.

Prudential Financial currently trades on a P/E of 9.78x. That sits below the Insurance industry average P/E of 11.65x and also below the peer average of 16.61x, suggesting the market is applying a lower earnings multiple than these broad benchmarks.

Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for Prudential Financial is 12.93x. This is a proprietary view of what the P/E might be given factors such as the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and key risks. Because it is tailored to the company’s own fundamentals rather than broad group averages, it can offer a more company specific reference point than simple peer or industry comparisons.

Comparing the Fair Ratio of 12.93x with the actual P/E of 9.78x indicates that, on this metric, Prudential Financial appears undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Prudential Financial Narrative

Earlier the article mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. This is where Narratives come in, giving you a simple story to attach to your numbers. You can spell out your view on Prudential Financial’s future revenue, earnings and margins, link that story to a financial forecast, and arrive at your own fair value that you can easily compare with today’s share price to judge whether it looks high or low. All of this can be done within Simply Wall St’s Community page, which millions of investors use. Each Narrative automatically refreshes as new earnings, news or analyst updates arrive. One investor might build a more optimistic Prudential Financial view closer to the US$124 high analyst value, while another might anchor on a more cautious stance nearer the US$91 low. You can then see clearly how those different stories lead to different fair values and, ultimately, different decisions about when to buy or sell.

data.description:
• For readers wondering whether First Solar at around US$195 has already run its course or still offers value, this article walks through what the current price might be implying about the stock.
  • The share price has pulled back around 2.8% over the last week and 2.1% over the last month, but still reflects a 56.9% return over the past year and 133.4% over five years, alongside a 28.8% decline year to date and a 10.5% decline over three years.
  • Recent headlines have focused on broad interest in solar and semiconductor related names, as well as ongoing attention on companies involved in renewable energy supply chains. This backdrop helps explain why investors have been reassessing both the potential and the risks around First Solar's current share price.
  • According to Simply Wall St's valuation checks, First Solar scores 6 out of 6 for being undervalued across several metrics. The next sections will unpack those methods and will also point to a more complete way to think about valuation that ties everything together at the end of the article.

Find out why First Solar's 56.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: First Solar Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF model, estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to their value in today's dollars.

For First Solar, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections expressed in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $507.9 million. Analysts provide free cash flow estimates out to 2030, with Simply Wall St extrapolating beyond the explicit analyst window to build a ten year path. This includes a projected free cash flow of $3.1b in 2030.

When all of those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated fair value of about $267.39 per share. Against a share price in the region of $195, this implies the stock is around 27.0% undervalued based on these cash flow assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for First Solar.

Approach 2: First Solar Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. This makes it a straightforward check on whether the current share price looks stretched or reasonable.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E depends a lot on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

First Solar is trading on a P/E of 13.7x, compared with an industry average of 42.2x for semiconductor stocks and a peer average of 79.5x. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 35.3x, which reflects what its P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, size and risk profile.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for the specific characteristics of the business rather than assuming all companies deserve similar multiples.

With a current P/E of 13.7x versus a Fair Ratio of 35.3x, the multiple based view suggests the shares are trading below that Fair Ratio.

Result: UNDERVALUED

The platform then translates that into a forecast and fair value that update when new results or news arrive.

This means two investors can both look at First Solar at around US$195, with one Narrative built on higher growth and margin assumptions and another based on more cautious figures. data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Astronics (ATRO) After Its 235% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Astronics at US$76.26 still offers value after a big run, the starting point is understanding what the current price actually reflects about the business.
  • The stock has recorded returns of 4.7% over the last 7 days, 11.0% over 30 days, 34.7% year to date and 234.6% over the past year, with 3 year and 5 year returns of 379.6% and 342.9% respectively. This naturally raises questions about how much optimism is already priced in.
  • Recent coverage around Astronics has focused on its position within the aerospace and defense supply chain and how market sentiment has shifted toward companies linked to that sector. These developments give extra context to the strong share price performance and help explain why more investors are paying attention now.
  • Even with this backdrop, Astronics records a valuation score of 1 out of 6. The key question is how different valuation approaches, and a broader way of thinking about value at the end of this article, might change how you see the stock.

Astronics scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Astronics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model looks at the cash Astronics is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the business might be worth now.

For Astronics, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest trailing twelve month free cash flow is about $57.3 million. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations point to free cash flow of $68.9 million in 2026 and $94.3 million in 2027, with Simply Wall St extending those projections out to 2035. By discounting this ten year stream of cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $90.53 per share.

Against the current share price of $76.26, this implies Astronics trades at roughly a 15.8% discount to the DCF estimate. Based on these cash flow assumptions, the model interprets this as the stock being undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Astronics is undervalued by 15.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Astronics.

Approach 2: Astronics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that support that price. It gives a quick sense of how many years of current earnings the market is effectively pricing in.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk usually justify a higher multiple, while more uncertainty or weaker growth prospects tend to pull it down.

Astronics currently trades on a P/E of 92.71x. That is well above the aerospace and defense industry average of 38.84x and also above the peer group average of 29.68x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Astronics is 60.79x. This proprietary metric estimates a company-specific P/E that reflects factors such as earnings growth characteristics, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk profile.

The Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for Astronics’ own financial and risk profile. Comparing the actual 92.71x P/E with the 60.79x Fair Ratio suggests the shares trade at a premium to what this framework would imply.

Result: OVERVALUED

On Simply Wall St's Community page, Narratives are set up so you can see and adjust the numbers behind a story. The platform updates those Narratives as new news, guidance or earnings on Astronics arrive, so your valuation view does not stay static while the facts change.

Another might sit closer to the lower Fair Value of US$32.75, reflecting more cautious expectations for revenue growth and profitability.   • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 145.9x compared with a peer average of 35.1x and a JP Hospitality industry average of 22.9x, even though trailing net income is only ¥155 million on ¥40,426 million of revenue.
  • Critics highlight that this rich multiple sits awkwardly with the recent profit record rather than being clearly backed by it:
    • The share price of ¥1,991 is also far above the DCF fair value estimate of ¥212.27, which points to a large gap between market pricing and the cash flow based valuation provided here.
    • With earnings growth over the past five years reported at about a 3% annual decline, the high P/E and premium to DCF fair value look more like a valuation risk than a reflection of strong growth in the recent numbers.

Quarterly Profits Improve After Prior Loss Year

  • Across the last six reported quarters, net income moved from a loss of ¥238 million in Q4 FY 2025 to positive figures of ¥137 million, ¥99 million, and ¥157 million in FY 2026 Q1 to Q3 on revenues ranging from ¥9,512 million to ¥10,631 million.
  • Optimistic investors might see this as a sign of steadier operations, but the broader record puts some limits around that bullish take:
    • Trailing twelve month net income of ¥155 million is much smaller than the ¥888 million reported a year earlier on ¥37,335 million of revenue, which means the recent profitable quarters still sit inside a weaker full year picture.
    • The combination of thin 0.4% trailing margin and the ¥687.0 million one off loss means any bullish story around improving quarterly EPS and net income has to account for how sensitive overall profitability remains to unusual items.
